suggestions.name: MarkerRegistry
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive marker management system that maintains collections of markers organized by layer and index. Extracting them into a MarkerRegistry class creates a focused component responsible for marker lifecycle operations (add, remove, clear, retrieve) while reducing the complexity of the CategoryPlot class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of managing markers on a chart plot. They handle the complete lifecycle of markers: adding, removing, clearing, retrieving, and drawing both domain and range markers. The methods manage markers across different layers (foreground/background) and different indices, providing a comprehensive marker management system.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "marker management" domain within chart plotting. They operate on four related data structures (foregroundDomainMarkers, backgroundDomainMarkers, foregroundRangeMarkers, backgroundRangeMarkers) and provide consistent operations across both domain and range marker types. This represents a cohesive subsystem for marker lifecycle management.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates marker management logic from other plot concerns
- Code reusability: Marker management could be reused by other plot types
- Complexity reduction: Removes ~20 methods from the CategoryPlot class, significantly simplifying it
- Testability: Marker operations can be tested independently without plot rendering complexity
- Maintainability: Changes to marker behavior are localized to one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate marker management concerns. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods manage markers) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on plot-specific functionality). It implements the Single Responsibility Principle by focusing solely on marker lifecycle management.

suggestions.name: DomainAxisLocationRegistry
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for registering and managing domain axis locations within a plot. They share a common data structure (domainAxisLocations map) and provide controlled access with validation and notification capabilities. Extracting them creates a focused registry component that encapsulates axis positioning logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of managing the location/positioning of domain axes within a chart plot. All four methods are overloaded variants that ultimately delegate to setting axis locations in a map, with different parameter combinations for index, location, and notification behavior.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "axis location management" domain concept. They encapsulate the logic for storing, validating, and updating where domain axes are positioned within the plot area. The domainAxisLocations field serves as the storage mechanism, while the methods provide controlled access with validation and notification capabilities.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates axis location management from other plot concerns
- Code reusability: The axis location logic could be reused by other plot types or chart components
- Complexity reduction: Removes axis positioning complexity from the main CategoryPlot class
- Testability: Axis location logic can be tested independently with focused unit tests

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all deal with axis location management) and can operate with low coupling (minimal dependencies on the parent plot class, mainly for notification).

suggestions.name: CategoryDomainZoomRestrictor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit that enforces the restriction that category plots cannot zoom their domain axis. Extracting them into a specialized class clarifies this behavioral constraint and separates zoom policy from core plotting functionality.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: handling domain axis zoom operations for a CategoryPlot. All three methods are overloaded versions of zoomDomainAxes with different parameters, but they all contain the same implementation - they do nothing because category plots cannot zoom their domain axis (which represents discrete categories rather than continuous values).

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "zoom interaction" domain, specifically for domain axis zooming. They represent a cohesive behavioral constraint where domain zooming is explicitly disabled for category-based plots. This is a specialized zoom behavior that differs from other plot types that may support domain zooming.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates zoom behavior logic from the main plotting functionality
- Code clarity: Makes it explicit that this is a no-op zoom implementation for category plots
- Polymorphism support: Could enable different zoom strategies for different plot types
- Testability: Zoom behavior can be tested independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Strategy pattern where different plot types can have different zoom behaviors. The Extract Class refactoring would create a specialized zoom handler that encapsulates the "no domain zoom" policy for category plots, improving separation of concerns.
